From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Defines two sets of codes for a number of writing systems

**ISO 15924**, ***Codes for the representation of names of scripts***, is an [international standard](/wiki/ISO_standard "ISO standard") defining codes for [writing systems](/wiki/Writing_systems "Writing systems") or scripts (a "set of graphic characters used for the written form of one or more languages"). Each script is given both a four-letter code and a numeric code.[[1]](#cite_note-ISO-15924:2004-1)

Where possible the codes are derived from [ISO 639-2](/wiki/ISO_639-2 "ISO 639-2"), where the name of a script and the name of a language using the script are identical (example: [Gujarātī](/wiki/Gujarati_script "Gujarati script") ISO 639 guj, ISO 15924 Gujr). Preference is given to the 639-2 Bibliographical codes, which is different from the otherwise often preferred use of the Terminological codes.[[1]](#cite_note-ISO-15924:2004-1)

4-letter ISO 15924 codes are incorporated into the [IANA Language Subtag Registry](/wiki/IANA_Language_Subtag_Registry "IANA Language Subtag Registry") for [IETF language tags](/wiki/IETF_language_tag "IETF language tag") and so can be used in file formats that make use of such language tags. For example, they can be used in [HTML](/wiki/HTML "HTML") and [XML](/wiki/XML "XML") to help [Web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") determine which [typeface](/wiki/Typeface "Typeface") to use for foreign text. This way one could differentiate, for example, between [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language") written in the [Cyrillic](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script") (`sr-Cyrl`) or [Latin](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") (`sr-Latn`) script, or mark [romanized](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") or [transliterated](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") text as such.

## Maintenance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_15924&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Maintenance")]

ISO appointed the [Unicode Consortium](/wiki/Unicode_Consortium "Unicode Consortium") as the [Registration Authority](/wiki/Registration_Authority "Registration Authority") (RA) for the standard. The RA is responsible for appointing a registrar who works with a Joint Advisory Committee (JAC) in developing and implementing the standard. The registrar from 2004 to 2018 was [Michael Everson](/wiki/Michael_Everson "Michael Everson"), and from January 2019 the registrar has been Markus Scherer of [Google](/wiki/Google "Google").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The JAC consists of six members: one representative of the RA (Markus Scherer), one representative of [ISO 639-2](/wiki/ISO_639-2 "ISO 639-2") (Randall K. Barry of the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress")), one representative of [ISO/TC 37](/wiki/ISO/TC_37 "ISO/TC 37") (Christian Galinski), one representative of [ISO/TC 46](/wiki/ISO/TC_46 "ISO/TC 46") (Peeter Päll), and two representatives of [ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 2](/wiki/ISO/IEC_JTC_1/SC_2 "ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 2") (Rick McGowan and Jan Kučera, both affiliated with the Unicode Consortium).[[3]](#cite_note-JAC_info-3)

## Script codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_15924&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Script codes")]

### Numeric ranges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_15924&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Numeric ranges")]

* 000–099 [Hieroglyphic](/wiki/Hieroglyphic "Hieroglyphic") and [cuneiform](/wiki/Cuneiform "Cuneiform") scripts
* 100–199 [Right-to-left alphabetic](/wiki/Right-to-left_alphabetic "Right-to-left alphabetic") scripts
* 200–299 [Left-to-right alphabetic](/wiki/Left-to-right_alphabetic "Left-to-right alphabetic") scripts
* 300–399 [Alphasyllabic](/wiki/Alphasyllabary "Alphasyllabary") scripts
* 400–499 [Syllabic](/wiki/Syllabary "Syllabary") scripts
* 500–599 [Ideographic](/wiki/Ideogram "Ideogram") scripts
* 600–699 [Undeciphered scripts](/wiki/Undeciphered_writing_systems "Undeciphered writing systems")
* 700–799 [Shorthands](/wiki/Shorthand "Shorthand") and other notations[[4]](#cite_note-4)
* 800–899 (unassigned)
* 900–999 [Private use](/wiki/Private_Use_Areas "Private Use Areas"), alias, special codes[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Special codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_15924&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Special codes")]

* Qaaa–Qabx (900–949): 50 Codes reserved for private use (for example, Qaag is defined in [LDML](/wiki/LDML "LDML") to mark [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language") text encoded for the [Zawgyi font](/wiki/Zawgyi_font "Zawgyi font"))[[6]](#cite_note-TR35-6)
* Zsye 993: [Emoji](/wiki/Emoji "Emoji")
* Zinh 994: Code for inherited script[[a]](#cite_note-7)
* Zmth 995: [Mathematical notation](/wiki/Mathematical_notation "Mathematical notation")
* Zsym 996: [Symbols](/wiki/Symbols "Symbols")
* Zxxx 997: Code for unwritten documents
* Zyyy 998: Code for undetermined script
* Zzzz 999: Code for uncoded script

### Exceptionally reserved codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_15924&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Exceptionally reserved codes")]

Two four-letter codes are reserved at the request of the [Common Locale Data Repository](/wiki/Common_Locale_Data_Repository "Common Locale Data Repository") (CLDR) project:[[7]](#cite_note-codelist-8)

* Root: Reserved for the language-neutral base of the CLDR locale tree
* True: Reserved for the [Boolean value](/wiki/Boolean_value "Boolean value") "true"

## Relations to other standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_15924&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Relations to other standards")]

The following standards are referred to as indispensable by ISO 15924.

* [ISO 639-2](/wiki/ISO_639-2 "ISO 639-2"):1998 Codes for the representation of names of languages — Part 2: Alpha-3 code
* ISO/IEC 9541-1:1991 Information technology — Font information interchange — Part 1: Architecture
* [ISO/IEC 10646-1:2020](/wiki/ISO/IEC_10646-1:2000(E) "ISO/IEC 10646-1:2000(E)") Information technology — Universal Multiple-Octet Coded Character Set (UCS)

For definition of font and glyph the standard refers to

* ISO/IEC 9541-1:1991

Around 160 scripts are defined in **[Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode")**. Through a linkpin called "Property Value Alias", Unicode has made a 1:1 connection between a script defined, and its ISO 15924 standard. See [Script (Unicode)](/wiki/Script_(Unicode) "Script (Unicode)").

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISO_15924&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** According to the [Unicode Standard, Annex #24, version 13.0.0](http://www.unicode.org/reports/tr24/tr24-31.html#Script_Values) `Inherited` is the Unicode script property value of "characters that may be used with multiple scripts, and that inherit their script from a preceding base character. These include nonspacing combining marks and enclosing combining marks, as well as U+200C ZERO WIDTH NON-JOINER and U+200D ZERO WIDTH JOINER." The value `Inherited` is described as matching the ISO 15924 code `Zinh`.
